coredata.dc:title: Effective enhancement of electron migration and photocatalytic performance of nitrogen-rich carbon nitride by constructing fungal carbon dot/molybdenum disulfide cocatalytic system

coredata.dc:description: To find a cocatalyst that can replace noble metals, fungal carbon dot (CD) modified molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) cocatalyst system was designed. The composites were prepared by hydrothermal and calcination methods with different ratios of CDs, MoS2 and nitrogen-rich carbon nitride (p-C3N5). p-C3N5 has excellent electronic properties, and MoS2 modified by CDs (D-MoS2) can significantly enhance the photocatalytic performance of p-C3N5 by improving the photogenerated electron migration efficiency. The experiments showed that the developed CDs/MoS2/C3N5 composites exhibited excellent performance in both photocatalytic hydrogen (H2) evolution and methylene blue (MB) degradation, with CMSCN5 (D-MoS2 with 5% mass fraction) showing the best photocatalytic activity. The corresponding H2 evolution rate of CMSCN5 was 444 μmol g-1h−1 and 1.45 times higher than that of unmodified p-C3N5, by 120 min, the removal rate of MB was up to 93.51%. The 5 cycle tests showed that CMSCN5 had great stability. The high charge mobility and high density of H2 evolution active sites of MoS2 nanosheets, together with the electron storage and transfer properties of CDs can obviously improve electron migration and reduce the photogenerated carrier recombination on the p-C3N5 surface. The design and preparation of such composites offer broad prospects for the development of photocatalytic systems with noble metal-free cocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Effective enhancement of electron migration and photocatalytic performance of nitrogen-rich carbon nitride by constructing fungal carbon dot/molybdenum disulfide cocatalytic system

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology, Material Science and Engineering College, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology, Material Science and Engineering College, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To find a cocatalyst that can replace noble metals, fungal carbon dot (CD) modified molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) cocatalyst system was designed. The composites were prepared by hydrothermal and calcination methods with different ratios of CDs, MoS2 and nitrogen-rich carbon nitride (p-C3N5). p-C3N5 has excellent electronic properties, and MoS2 modified by CDs (D-MoS2) can significantly enhance the photocatalytic performance of p-C3N5 by improving the photogenerated electron migration efficiency. The experiments showed that the developed CDs/MoS2/C3N5 composites exhibited excellent performance in both photocatalytic hydrogen (H2) evolution and methylene blue (MB) degradation, with CMSCN5 (D-MoS2 with 5% mass fraction) showing the best photocatalytic activity. The corresponding H2 evolution rate of CMSCN5 was 444 μmol g-1h−1 and 1.45 times higher than that of unmodified p-C3N5, by 120 min, the removal rate of MB was up to 93.51%. The 5 cycle tests showed that CMSCN5 had great stability. The high charge mobility and high density of H2 evolution active sites of MoS2 nanosheets, together with the electron storage and transfer properties of CDs can obviously improve electron migration and reduce the photogenerated carrier recombination on the p-C3N5 surface. The design and preparation of such composites offer broad prospects for the development of photocatalytic systems with noble metal-free cocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industrialization and society, human beings are facing the increasingly serious challenge of energy depletion and environmental pollution [1,2].

Photocatalysis technology for pollutant degradation and decomposition of water are currently important strategies for remediating the environment and producing green energy such as H2 [3–6]. The photocatalytic reaction process in a typical semiconductor consists of three main steps: (1) the semiconductor absorbs light and generates electron-hole pairs; (2) the charges separate and migrate to the semiconductor surface; and (3) the surface activation for redox reaction. The efficiency of photocatalysis is limited by the rapid recombination of carriers and the low activation capacity of the reacting molecular surface, which reduce the charge amount available for catalytic reactions. Cocatalysts are combined with semiconductors to avoid the above, which can both extract charges from the semiconductor surface and act as active sites for molecules. Noble metals (Pt et al.) are the most common cocatalysts at present for photocatalytic H2 evolution[7,8]. However, the noble metals cannot meet the demand of large-scale commercialization due to their scarcity and high cost. Therefore, it is essential to design a widely sourced, inexpensive and readily available cocatalyst to replace noble metals, which is important to enhance future economic development in the photocatalytic field.

In recent years, alternative noble metal-free cocatalysts, such as MoS2 have been developed[9,10]. MoS2 is composed of intercalated S-Mo-S layers, and is an ideal candidate for H2 evolution due to its high H2 activation capacity[11–13]. From previous studies, it was found that the basal plane of MoS2 was catalytically inert and its main catalytic activity came mainly from the active sites at the edge of the lamellae layer[14]. These properties greatly limit the practical application of MoS2 as a photocatalytic reaction cocatalyst. To overcome this limitation, CDs can be introduced into MoS2. CDs are a new type of small (<10 nm) carbon nanomaterial with great abilities of significant electron transfer and electron storage, which can facilitate electron transfer and transport efficiently. In previous reports on CD modification of MoS2, the carbon sources of CDs were usually chemical sources[15,16] and graphene[17], which cannot meet the needs of environmental protection and low cost. Therefore, biomass should be considered as a carbon source for CD preparation due to its wide range of sources, environmental friendliness, and excellent performance with optical properties and electrical conductivity (e.g. Corncob[18], straw[19], pear juice[20] and sycamore leaves[21] have been reported as source materials of CD synthesis). Fungal fibers are filamentous materials rich in proteins, amino acids and polysaccharides. A large amount of biomass waste rich in fungal fibers will be produced in the process of edible fungal production as well as human life. It may be further developed as a carbon source for synthesizing CDs, which can not only reduce chemical pollution, but also improve the utilization rate and economic value. To combine the good electron storage and conduction properties of biomass CDs with the high H2 activation capacity of MoS2, CD/MoS2 hybrids can be designed as cocatalysts. The CD/MoS2 cocatalysts will be integrated with semiconductor catalysts instead of noble metals to improve the efficiency of photocatalytic reactions.

Semiconductor graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) now has now been extensively studied in photocatalysis due to its chemical stability and low cost[22,23]. However, the larger energy band gap (Eg = 2.7 eV) still limits the use of visible light, and tuning the C/N ratio is an effective way to improve the energy band structure[24,25]. The presence of N-rich azo and heptazine groups in the structure of N-rich carbon nitride (C3N5) gives it superior electronic properties compared with g-C3N4 [25,26]. Therefore, it is of significance to develop C3N5 materials with special structures to improve their photocatalytic performance. In recent years, Mane et al.[24] prepared mesoporous C3N5 nanostructures with high N content and large specific surface area showing excellent performance in photocatalysis using the hard template method. Greener synthesis methods have also been developed to reduce the use of hazardous etchant HF in the preparation process. Zhang et al.[27] proposed a new synthesis strategy for the preparation of rod-like mesoporous g-C3N5 using KBr as a guiding agent. In summary, the development of C3N5 materials for photocatalytic applications is of great importance.

In this work, we exploited a fungal CD/MoS2 cocatalyst for the first time and integrated it with C3N5 to improve the photocatalytic efficiency of the semiconductor C3N5. The fungal CD/MoS2 cocatalyst can extract charge from the C3N5 surface and act as active sites for the reaction, and the CDs also play an important role as a bridge for charge transport in this structure. A series of CD/MoS2/C3N5 composites were prepared by a hydrothermal method and high temperature calcination (Fig. 1 a). The morphologies, structures, chemical states and catalytic properties of composite photocatalysts were characterized in detail. The photocatalytic activities of the prepared CD/MoS2/C3N5 composites in H2 evolution and MB degradation were studied. The CD-modified MoS2 as a cocatalyst effectively improved the electron migration and separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers of C3N5 in the composite, thus enhancing the photocatalytic activity.

Sodium sulfite anhydrous (Na2SO3), acid Fuchsin (AF), l-cysteine and l-lysine were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China. Sodium sulfate anhydrous (Na2SO4) and methylene blue (MB) were supplied from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China, and crystal violet was purchased from Tianjin Tianxin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China. Methanol (MeOH), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), malachite green (MG) and Rhodamine B (Rh B) were procured from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China. 3-amino-1,2,4-triazole (3-AT), sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4·2H2O), furfuryl alcohol (FFA) and Sodium sulfide (Na2S·9H2O) were procured from 9 Ding Chemistry, Shanghai Yindian, Shanghai Zhanyun and Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China, respectively. Fungal fibers and deionized water were made in our laboratory. All chemicals were analytically pure and brought from different companies without any further purification.

The morphologies of photocatalysts were researched by transmission electron microscope (TEM) at 12.5 kV (Quanta200, FEI, Netherlands), high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) at a 200 kV acceleration voltage (TEM-2100, LJEMOC, Japan). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was acquired at a resolution of 4 cm−1 scanning from 4000 to 400 cm−1 (iS10, Nicolet, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) used non monochromatic Al X-rays as the primary excitation (PHI5700, Thermo Electron Corporation, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed under Cu Kα irradiation (40 kV, 30 mA) with a scanning range of 5-85° at a speed of 5°/min (XRD-6100, Shimadzu, Japan). The specific surface areas and pore-size distributions were studied by the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method on a nitrogen adsorption analyzer (ASAP2020, Micromeritics, USA). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were scanned in the range of 200–800 nm using BaSO4 as a reference (TU-1900, Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd. China). Photoluminescence (PL, G9800A, Agilent, USA) spectra were measured with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), Schottky curve and photocurrent transient response (I-t) were executed in a system of three-electrode, Na2SO4 solution (0.25 M) served as the electrolyte. Electron spin resonance (ESR) was measured to capture radicals using 5,5′-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) as spin trap reagents during photocatalytic degradation (JES FA200, JEOL Co, Japan). A 300-W Xenon light was used as light source (Hxuv300 Visref, Beijing CEJ Tech. Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). Gas chromatography (GC-7920, Beijing CEJ Tech. Co., Ltd., Beijing, China) with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) was used for the quantitative determination of H2. The total organic carbon (TOC) content before and after MB photodegradation was determined on TOC analyzer (TOC-L Shimazu Co, Japan).

The fungal CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method reported by our group[28]. The CD/MoS2 was also prepared by hydrothermal method[29]. Na2MoO4·2H2O (0.0039 M) and l-cysteine (0.012 M) were dissolved in DI water with constant stirring to form a uniform solution and then 50 mL CDs (0.10 mg mL−1) aqueous solution were added. The mixture was poured into the stainless steel autoclave with Teflon-lined and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. After the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature, the black sediment was washed several times with deionized water and ethanol and dried in vacuum oven. Finally, the mass ratio CD/MoS2 was 0.81% black solid (named D-MoS2), which was ground into powder in the mortar. The prepared D-MoS2 were dispersed under ultrasonic treatment to obtain 1 mg mL−1 D-MoS2 suspension. Using the same method as above, CD solution was not added to obtain black solid MoS2, which was ground into powder.

The synthesis process of p-C3N5 was based on previous reports with some modifications[27]. 3-amino-1,2,4-triazole powder (1.50 g) was added in an Na2SO4 aqueous solution (10 g Na2SO4 in 30 mL DI water). The mixture was then heated in a water bath at 80 °C with constant stirring until almost all of the solids were precipitated and the obtained sediments were dried overnight at 60 °C. The dried powder was kept in a crucible and heated at 500 °C for 3 h in a N2 atmosphere at a ramp rate of 10 °C min−1. After cooling naturally, the products were washed many times with DI water and remove the residual water using hot air oven at 60 °C to get p-C3N5. g-C3N5 was obtained by heating 3-amino-1,2,4- triazole (3 g) for 3 h at 500 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 in N2 atmosphere. A certain amount of urea was placed in a crucible, calcined at 500 °C for 2 h in air and then heated to 550 °C, and kept for 2 h to prepare g-C3N4.

Firstly, 1 g p-C3N5 was dispersed in 20 mL DI water, then a certain amount (10 mL, 30 mL, 50 mL, 70 mL, 90 mL) of the D-MoS2 suspension (1 mg/mL) was added into the p-C3N5 suspension under constant stirring. After stirring for 1 h and ultrasonic dispersing for 2 h, the mixture was dried in oven. The dried solids were thoroughly ground and heated at 300 °C for 2 h under N2 atmosphere to obtain 1%, 3%, 5%, 7%, 9% D-MoS2 mass fraction D-MoS2/p-C3N5 composites, which were written as CMSCNx (x = 1, 3, 5, 7, 9), respectively. Similarly, the D-MoS2 suspension was replaced with MoS2 suspension to prepare MoS2/p-C3N5 binary composite named as MSCN5, in which the mass fraction of MoS2 is 5%.

Typically, 50 mg as-obtained photocatalyst was dispersed in Na2S and Na2SO3 (100 mL, 0.35 M) solution with constant stirring. Prior to light irradiation, a vacuum was created to remove dissolved O2 from the solution and the temperature of reaction system was hold at 279 K. The H2 evolution reaction was taken place in Pyrex flask using a 300-W Xenon light and the generated H2 was collected by gas chromatography with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) intermittently and automatically. In addition, the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) was evaluated at 507 nm and calculated using the following equation:
(1)

The photocatalytic activity of catalysts was evaluated under 300-W xenon lamp (λ ≥ 420 nm) irradiation using MB solution as a model substrate. Briefly, 10 mg C3N5 and CMSCN composites were added to 50 mL 30 mg L−1 MB solution with stirring for 30 min in the dark before light irradiation to reach adsorption equilibrium. After a certain time interval, 3 mL suspension was extracted, centrifuged to obtain a clarified solution, and the absorbance of MB solution was measured at 664 nm using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. Control experiments were performed with single MoS2 or D-MoS2 and dosages were the same as D-MoS2 content in CMSCN5. The chemical oxygen demand (COD) values of MB solution before and after photodegradation were determined by dichromate method (HJ 828–2017).

The structures and micromorphologies of the CDs, MoS2, D-MoS2, p-C3N5, and CMSCN composites were analyzed by SEM and TEM. Fig. 1b shows that MoS2 has a clear laminar structure consisting of many sheets and that the MoS2 nanosheet edges curl to form a flower-like structure[15,30]. The typical stacked nanosheet structure of MoS2 and the ultrathin nanosheet more contribute to exposing many edges with active sites. The SEM images of D-MoS2 are similar to those of MoS2, and a lamellar stacking structure can also be observed (Fig. 1c). From Fig. 1d, it can be observed that p-C3N5 is an irregularly shaped polymer with a pore structure on the surface. The morphology of the CMSCN composite is also irregular, and the small particles aggregated on the surface are considered to be the cocatalyst D-MoS2 (Fig. 1e). The EDS results demonstrated the distribution of C, N, S and Mo elements in the CMSCN composite, indicating that the CD/MoS2/p-C3N5 ternary composite catalysts with relatively low Mo and S elements were successfully produced (Fig. 1f).

It can be seen from the Fig. S1 shows that the morphological characteristics of the SEM and TEM images of different samples correspond to each other. TEM images show a narrow size distribution of CDs with almost ellipsoidal shapes (Fig. 2 a), and a carbon lattice stripe spacing of 0.21 nm is observed with HRTEM (Fig. S2a). Consistent with the nature of graphitized C, it shows the graphitized nature of CDs. Clear lattice stripes can be observed in the HRTEM image of MoS2, and the lattice stripes with a face spacing of 0.63 nm may be attributed to the (002) crystal planes of MoS2. In addition, the spacing of lattice fringes is 0.27 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal planes of MoS2 (Fig. 2b)[30,31]. The CDs and MoS2 are observed directly from the HRTEM image (Fig. 2c), indicating the successful combination of nanostructured D-MoS2. The fast Fourier transform of the CDs shows an obvious six-membered ring structure (inset in Fig. 2a)[32], and the same six-membered ring is also present in the D-MoS2 composite (inset in Fig. 2c), which further proves that CDs are introduced into D-MoS2. The TEM images of p-C3N5 show irregular layered amorphous structures without lattices, while defective pore structures can be observed on the surface of p-C3N5 that are left after the salt is eluted (Fig. S2b and S2c). p-C3N5, MoS2 and CDs can be clearly observed in Fig. 2d, proving the formation of a ternary composite. p-C3N5 has no lattice structure, while lattice stripes with face spacings of 0.63 nm and 0.21 nm correspond to MoS2 and CDs, respectively.

To investigate the crystal structure information of the catalysts, XRD was used to characterize the catalysts. The inset in Fig. 3 a shows the patterns of CDs, MoS2, and D-MoS2. The characteristic diffraction peaks of CDs centered at 20.5° are indexed to the (002) plane of graphitic C. Both MoS2 and D-MoS2 have diffraction peaks at 10°, 33° and 58° corresponding to the (002), (100) and (110) crystal faces of MoS2, indicating the formation of MoS2 in the D-MoS2 composites[33]. There is little difference in the diffraction pattern of the MoS2 and D-MoS2 samples, and it is difficult to observe the characteristic peaks of carbon diffraction in the D-MoS2 samples due to the low amount of carbon and relatively low diffraction intensity. The (100) peak in D-MoS2 shows disordered laminar flow, which is consistent with the characterization results of SEM and TEM. According to JCPDS 87–1526 card, the diffraction peak of p-C3N5 at 13.4° is associated with the (100) crystal plane, indicating the in-plane structural ordering of p-C3N5 [34]. The strong peak at 27.2° (002) belongs to the interlayer stacking peak of the aromatic system, which is indexed to the graphitized material. In the diffraction images of the CMSCN material, the 100 and 110 peak surfaces of D-MoS2 can be clearly observed at 33° and 58° (Fig. 3a), and the peak intensity increases with the mass of D-MoS2 content, indicating the successful preparation of D-MoS2/CN composites.

The functional groups of different samples were explored by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig S3a and S3b). The black trace in Fig. S3a corresponds to fungus biomass CDs. The absorption peaks at 2921 and 2863 cm−1 are attributed to CH stretching vibrations. The bands at 1643, 1577 and 1395 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations with CO, CN and CN, respectively. The broad peaks in the regions 3052–3624 and 1000–1126 cm−1 belong to the OH/NH and -SO3/CS groups, respectively. The band at 658 cm−1 is stretching vibrations of CS. The above results confirm the presence of N and S elements on CDs (probably from proteins in fungi). The FT-IR spectra of MoS2 and D-MoS2 are very similar, and the introduction of CDs cannot significantly change the surface functional group structure of MoS2. The spectral bands of D-MoS2 samples at 3450, 1620 and 1385 cm−1 are attributed to the peaks of OH, CO and CC groups, while the peaks at 1112 and 658 cm−1 are assigned to the C-OH and CS stretching vibrations[35–38]. The FT-IR spectra of p-C3N5 and CMSCN are similar (Fig. S3b), while the characteristic bands of D-MoS2 are hardly observed, indicating that the contents of D-MoS2 are low and that its peaks are masked by that of p-C3N5. The peaks at 3070 and 422 cm−1 are related to stretching vibrational absorption peaks of –NH2 and triazole rings[39], respectively. The absorption bands in the ranges of 1680–1690 and 1300–1270 cm−1 are attributed to stretching/deformation of cross-linked triazole rings and stretching vibrational modes of CN bonds[40]. For the p-C3N5 and CMSCN composite samples, a new peak at 2177 cm−1 is related to the asymmetric tensile vibration of CN[41,42]. The peak intensity at 3100 cm−1 of the NH bands of p-C3N5 is reduced compared to that of g-C3N5, which suggests that the NH groups gradually decrease and CN gradually increase during the preparation of C3N5 induced with Na2SO4. The possible reason is that during the condensation of the triazole rings, the Na2SO4 salt reacts with the amine group of g-C3N5, resulting in the formation of CN.

The chemical states of different elements in the D-MoS2, p-C3N5 and CMSCN5 composites were determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The XPS survey spectrum shows that CMSCN5 contained C, N, O, S, and Mo elements, which corresponds to the EDS results (Fig. S3c). p-C3N5 contains mainly 56.44% C, 33.17 % N, and a trace amount of O. The N/C ratio is approximately 1.7, indicating the successful synthesis of C3N5 (Table S1). The XPS spectrum of p-C3N5 is observed with peaks of C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s at 287.6, 399.5 and 533.8 eV, respectively[43]. Elemental analysis shows that CMSCN5 consists of 40.39% C, 44.93 % N, 10.33% O, 3.11% S and 1.25% Mo (Table S1). The Mo, S contents of the composite are very low. The peaks of C, N, O, Mo and S can all be found in the spectrum of CMSCN5 (Fig. S3c), indicating that C3N5, CDs and MoS2 are smoothly compounded in the CMSCN samples. The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3b) shows that CMSCN5 and p-C3N5 have two peaks at 284.6 and 287.9 eV and 284.1 and 287.4 eV, which are related to sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbon. sp3 hybridized carbon can be divided into edge group carbon and external carbon, and the stronger sp2peak can originate from NC-N[44–46]. Fig. 3c shows the N 1 s high-resolution spectra of p-C3N5 and CMSCN5, showing three peaks at 397.9, 399.3 and 403.8 eV, which are associated with -C-NC, tertiary N and π-π* bonds, respectively[47–50]. The π-π* bonds at 403.8 eV demonstrate the presence of triazole and CN layer graphitization stacking and the tertiary N indicates the presence of a triazine ring, which corresponds to the above FT-IR results, indicating the successful synthesis of p-C3N5 with a triazole structure. As seen from Fig. S3d, the O 1 s high-resolution spectrum of p-C3N5 has two peaks at 532.9 and 531.5 eV, corresponding to N-CO and C-OH, respectively, while the spectrum of CMSCN5 has three peaks at 533.7, 531.5 and 530.2 eV, associated with H2O adsorbed on the sample surface, C-OH and Mo-O[15,28,51]. There are six peaks in the high-resolution spectrum of D-MoS2 Mo 3 d (Fig. 3d) and the characteristic signals at 228.2 and 231.2 eV are attributed to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 of Mo4+. The presence of Mo6+ 3d orbitals due to incomplete decomposition of MoO4 2- can be observed at 232.0 and 235.0 eV, while Mo6+ partially reduces to Mo5+ with a peak at 229.5 eV. In addition, the characteristic peak at 225.4 eV is attributed to the S 2 s track of MoS2 [11,30,52–54]. The Mo 3d XPS spectrum of CMSCN5 has a similar peak distribution to that of D-MoS2, but the characteristic peak of Mo5+ is no longer observed. Mo5+ is converted to Mo6+ or Mo4+ when MoS2 further combines with C3N5 to form CMSCN with a high-temperature calcination process. The typical XPS peaks of S 2p for D-MoS2 and CMSCN5 are shown in Fig. 3e, which shows a shape approximately identical to that of D-MoS2 with peaks at 161.0 and 162.1 eV for the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 orbitals of S2 2-, respectively[54]. The peak at 163.6 eV is related to apical S2 - or bridging disulfide bonds (S2 2-), while both of them profitably adsorb hydrogen atoms and serve as highly active sites for the HER[29,30,55].

To further understand the physical structure information of p-C3N5, D-MoS2 and CMSCN, we obtained N2 adsorption–desorption curves, BET surface areas and BJH pore size distributions. As shown in Fig. 3f, the results for pristine g-C3N5, p-C3N5, MoS2, D-MoS2 and CMSCN exhibit the typical H3-type hysteresis loop of the IV isotherm, indicating the presence of mesoporous structures [56,57]. In addition, p-C3N5 (3.77 m2 g−1) has a larger surface area and pore size than pristine g-C3N5 (0.71 m2 g−1, Table 1 and Fig.S4), indicating that Na2SO4 elution increases the specific surface area and pore structure of C3N5. The pore size distribution shows the presence of a small amount of mesoporous and microporous structures in the catalysts. Based on these results, g-C3N5 and its composites with pore structures were successfully synthesized. Compared with p-C3N5, the D-MoS2 nanosheet in the composites led to a large change in the hysteresis line of CMSCN. The BET data of the prepared materials are presented in Table 1, of which MoS2 has a large specific surface area of 71.75 m2 g−1 due to its lamellar structure. However, doping of CDs on MoS2 causes a decrease in the D-MoS2 specific surface area (24.70 m2 g−1) by CDs aggregating on the MoS2 lamellar structure.

g-C3N5 and CMSCN were characterized by UV–vis DRS, and CMSCN showed a slight redshift compared to p-C3N5 (Fig. 4 a), which might be related to the introduction of D-MoS2. The ternary heterostructure has a longer visible wavelength than pristine p-C3N5. In addition, the band gaps of different catalysts are obtained according to the transformed Kubelka-Munk[58] function (Fig. 4b), and the band gaps of the original g-C3N5 and p-C3N5 are 1.83 and 1.81 eV, respectively. The band gap of CMSCN5 is 1.73 eV, and the lower band gap of CMSCN may be attributed to the nanosheet structure of D-MoS2 and the abundant N content in its framework. The unique structure provides a larger surface area and abundant active sites, which shortens the transport path of photocarriers from the bulk to the surface and inhibits the recombination of electron-hole pairs. To identify the energy band structures of the p-C3N5 and CMSCN composites further, photoelectrochemical measurements were performed. The Mott-Schottky curves for the p-C3N5 and CMSCN samples show linear regions with positive slopes, indicating n-type semiconductor behavior with electrons as the dominant carriers (Fig. 3c). The x-intercept of the linear region is used to calculate the flat-band potential (VFB), and the obtained results are converted into NHE potentials to further calculate the ECB for p-C3N5 and CMSCN Schottky contacts[59–61]. The flat-band potentials for the p-C3N5 and CMSCN composites are −0.47 and −0.44 V vs. Ag/AgCl (-0.27 and −0.24 eV vs. NHE (normal hydrogen electrode)), respectively. The ECBs of the p-C3N5 and CMSCN5 Schottky contacts are approximately −0.37 and −0.34 eV, respectively. Combining the band gap values obtained from Tauc plots, the valence band potentials (EVB) of p-C3N5 and CMSCN5 samples are calculated to be 1.44 and 1.39 eV, respectively[62,63].

When the catalyst is irradiated by solar energy, electrons on the photocatalyst VB are excited and leap to the CB. Then, the electrons move directionally and form a current under the action of an electric field. This means that the charges have better separation performance when the intensity of the photocurrent is higher. The photocurrent response of the samples with four switching cycles is shown in Fig. 4d. CMSCN5 has a higher photocurrent intensity than the other samples and a stronger ability to separate photoinduced electrons and holes[64]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were obtained for D-MoS2, p-C3N5 and CMSCN5. D-MoS2 has a significantly smaller Nyquist arc radius than p-C3N5 (Fig. 4e), and it can also be observed that the arc radius decreases similarly when D-MoS2 is added to p-C3N5 to form CMSCN composites. The introduction of D-MoS2 can result in lower charge transfer resistance and faster interfacial charge transfer rate of composites with greater potential for photocatalytic applications[65,66]. The interfacial charge transfer of catalysts was studied by measuring the excitation at 380 nm and recording the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum (Fig. 4f). PL is caused by photoelectron-hole complexation. The weaker the light intensity is, the lower the photoinduced carrier recombination rate[67]. The emission bands of the catalysts are at 400–450 nm, and the PL intensities of p-C3N5, MoS2 and CMSCN are all highest at approximately 424 nm. The luminescence intensity of MoS2 is lower than that of p-C3N5, and the observed PL intensity is significantly lower when D-MoS2 is added to p-C3N5, probably because D-MoS2 enhances electron migration and reduces the electron-hole combination[68]. CDs can absorb longer wavelength light to produce more active photons in photocatalytic reactions. The CDs act as photosensitizers to transport electrons and promote the separation of electrons and holes. The PL intensity decreases with the addition of D-MoS2, indicating that the ternary heterojunction composite accelerates the separation of photogenerated carriers.

The H2 evolution performance of the D-MoS2/p-C3N5 composite photocatalyst system without noble metal was evaluated in Na2S and Na2SO3 solutions for H2 evolution. The H2 evolution curves of CMSCN photocatalysts with different D-MoS2 contents are shown in Fig. 5 a and compared with those of pure p-C3N5, MoS2, and D-MoS2 photocatalysts. The pure p-C3N5 sample has photocatalytic activity with a H2 evolution amount of 1225 μmol g−1, which is due to the suitable energy band gap (1.81 eV) and unique electronic structure of g-C3N5. The H2 evolution amount of the D-MoS2 catalyst is 1086 μmol g−1, and the introduction of D-MoS2 into p-C3N5 results in significantly better H2 evolution performance. With the increase in D-MoS2 addition, the H2 evolution amount of CMSCN composites is further enhanced, and the highest H2 evolution amount of 1776 μmol g−1 is achieved by CMSCN5, which is approximately 1.45 times higher than p-C3N5. However, the H2 evolution activity decreases with a further increase in the D-MoS2 content. The decrease may be due to the addition of a mass of D-MoS2. Excess MoS2 shields the active sites on the CMSCN surface and reduces the light intensity. Therefore, an appropriate D-MoS2 content is essential to optimize the photocatalytic performance of CMSCN composites.

Fig. 5b shows a comparison of the H2 evolution rates for different samples. The H2 evolution rate is not high (189 μmol g-1h−1) when using MoS2 alone as the catalyst. This is because pure MoS2 mainly absorbs H+ at the edges and limits the occurrence of H2 evolution reactions. The doping of CDs on MoS2 increases the electron transport so that D-MoS2 (272 μmol g-1h−1) exhibits a higher H2 evolution rate than pure MoS2. The p-C3N5 exhibits low H2 evolution activity (306 μmol g-1h−1) because of the rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, while the H2 evolution rate of CMSCN5 is 444 μmol g-1h−1, which is much higher than that of the other samples. The AQE of CMSCN5 at 507 nm was 2.23%, which was 1.56 times that of p-C3N5. Comparing CMSCN5 with some photocatalysts reported to date, CMSCN5 shows better H2 evolution performance, even better than some cocatalytic systems with noble metals (Table S3). This is because D-MoS2 as a cocatalyst for p-C3N5 H2 evolution has the advantages of inhibiting charge recombination, improving interfacial electron migration, and increasing the quantity of reaction centers[29,68]. The electrons in the p-C3N5 layer can migrate to the MoS2 nanosheets through the CD particles, which prolongs the carrier lifetime and then reacts with the H+ adsorbed on the MoS2 surface to generate H2. In the composite, a tight interface is formed between MoS2 and p-C3N5, the heterogeneous structure promotes charge transfer between the two components, and CDs as electron acceptors enhance the separation efficiency of photoinduced carriers, thus promoting the photocatalytic H2 evolution performance. Therefore, p-C3N5 with the D-MoS2 cocatalyst exhibits rational H2 evolution activity.

To investigate the stability of the CMSCN5 catalyst, cyclic H2 evolution experiments were carried out, and Fig. 5c shows the evolution curve of the H2 during cyclic photocatalysis. The results showed that H2 evolution activity of CMSCN5 could still reach 68% after 20 h of irradiation, and the reduced H2 evolution activity might be mainly caused by the photocorrosion of MoS2. Fig. 5d and Fig. S5a show the TEM and X-ray diffraction pattern of CMSCN5 after cycling. No significant morphological or crystal structure changes occurred in the cycled CMSCN5 composite. The reduced H2 evolution activity may be mainly caused by the photocorrosion of MoS2. XPS analysis results of the catalyst after use are shown in Fig. 5e-5f and Fig.S5b-S5d. We observe remarkable changes in the state of Mo, S. The characteristic peaks of the Mo 3d5/2 orbitals are not observed in the Mo 3d high-resolution spectrum of the CMSCN5 used. The area of the peaks belonging to Mo6+ increases significantly, indicating that Mo4+ loses electrons and converts to Mo6+ during the photocatalytic reaction. The results are also reflected in the S 2p high-resolution spectra, where the 2p orbital of S2- disappears and the characteristic peak area of S4+ increases obviously. The results indicate that MoS2 undergoes photocorrosion and is partially oxidized in the photocatalytic reaction. The structure of C3N5 is relatively more stable after use, and the high-resolution spectra of N 1 s are almost unchanged. The sp2 hybridized C decreases and sp3 hybridized C increases in the spectra of C 1 s, indicating that some NC-N groups are converted to tertiary N. The O 1 s spectra show that C-OH groups on the catalyst surface are converted to N-CO after use, indicating that the hydroxyl groups of C3N5 are oxidized during the photocatalytic reaction.

The photocatalytic degradation performance of the composite catalyst on the dye was further investigated. For low concentrations of pollutants, it is more suitable to use first-order kinetic model to study the degradation process (Fig.S6 and Table S2). The first-order kinetic formula is as follows[69]:
(2)
ln
C
C 0
= - k t

The degradation rate constants k of the composite catalysts for several different dyes are shown in Fig. S7a includes Rh B, CV, MB, MG, AF and MO, where the composite catalyst showed the most significant degradation effect of MB. Zeta potential tests were performed on different samples and the results are shown in Fig. S7b. The p-C3N5, D-MoS2 and CMSCN samples show negative potentials. Rh B, CV, MG and MB are all cationic dyes that ionize cations in aqueous solution. The catalyst and the dyes are more easily combined through electrostatic interactions. The photocatalytic degradation activity of the prepared samples was studied using the most active MB as a model, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 a. The photocatalytic performance gradually increased from p-C3N5 to CMSCN5 but then decreased for CMSCN7 and CMSCN9. The photocatalytic degradation activity of the D-MoS2/p-C3N5 heterojunction was better than that of p-C3N5, MoS2, D-MoS2 and MoS2/p-C3N5. The first-order kinetic curves of different catalysts (Fig. 6b) and kinetic constants (Fig. 6c) were calculated according to Eq. (1), CMSCN5 showed the best photocatalytic effect and the reaction rate constant of the CMSCN5 sample (0.022 min−1) was 2.23 times higher than that of g-C3N5. The degradation rates corresponding to different catalysts are shown in Fig. S7c, and the degradation rate of CMSCN5 reached 93.51%. Meanwhile, we checked the TOC and COD values of MB solution before and after photocatalysis treatment by the CMSCN5 composite catalyst. The COD and TOC values of MB solution decreased by 312 mg/L and 16.37 mg/L after 2 h of visible photocatalytic treatment, respectively. The TOC and COD values decreased by 67.53% and 49.58%, respectively. Compared with other reported photocatalysts (Table S3), CMSCN5 photocatalytic degradation of MB is outstanding and exhibits a synergistic adsorption-catalysis effect for effective removal of pollutants.

It can be seen from Fig. 6a that the adsorption of D-MoS2 plays an obvious effect during the dark reaction because of the large specific surface area of the lamellar structure of MoS2, adsorbing the dye molecules on the surface of the catalyst and facilitating the occurrence of the light reaction. As mentioned above, the zeta potential on the catalyst surface is negative. Zeta potential is the electrical potential on the sliding plane, which is not equal to the electrical surface potential, and the sliding plane is the interface that separates the moving fluid from the fluid attached to the surface. The edges of the MoS2 nanosheets have a large amount of unsaturated S that can trap H+ in solution to form the Stern layer. Although the Stern layer contains a large amount of positive charge, its potential is still negative. The sliding plane with negative potential interacts with the dye that ionizes cations in the solution and promotes the adsorption of dye molecules on the surface of the Stern layer by the catalyst[70]. Moreover, the addition of MoS2 increases the reaction sites of the composite catalyst, the construction of MoS2 and C3N5 heterostructures effectively improves the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers, and CDs play important roles in electron transfer, which synergistically promotes the photocatalytic degradation performance. The cycling stability of the CMSCN5 catalyst was examined (Fig. 6d), and the photocatalytic degradation rate could still reach 87.03% after five cycles of reaction, indicating that the catalyst had great stability and reproducibility. TEM and PL tests were performed on the degraded MB solution, and no CDs were observed to be dissolved in the organic solution (Fig. S8).

In addition, different reagents were used to capture radicals to study the photodegradation mechanism of CMSCN5. p-C3N5 was the main photocatalyst for CMSCN5, while BQ, MeOH, FFA and EDTA were added to the reaction system to determine the effect of photocatalytic efficiency. It could be seen in Fig. S7d had the most significant inhibitory effect on MB degradation when the FFA sacrificial agent was added with a 25.63% reduction in the degradation rate, which showed that singlet oxygen (1O2) was the most important active radical in the photocatalytic degradation process. The photocatalytic efficiency does not change obviously with the addition of EDTA. This result implies that the vacancy (h+) is not the main functional active radical. However, the photocatalytic degradation rates were reduced by 13.78% and 16.44% in 60 min (Fig. S9) when MeOH and BQ were added to the catalytic system, respectively, which implied that hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH) and superoxide radical (⋅O2 –) also played roles in the photocatalytic process. To further understand the formation and transformation of active radicals during photocatalysis, ESR measurements were performed with DMPO and TEMP as spin-trap reagents. No active species signal was detected in the dark, but when the light was turned on, ESR signals for TEMP-1O2, DMPO-⋅OH and DMPO-⋅O2 – were found (Fig. 8 a-c). There are four characteristic peaks for DMPO-·OH under visible light with intensity height ratios of almost 1:2:2:1, which are consistent with the DMPO-⋅OH adducts[71]. The ESR results correspond with the active species capture experiments, indicating that CMSCN5 efficiently produces 1O2, ⋅O2 – and ⋅OH during photocatalysis. The orange traces in Fig. 7 show the possible processes for MB degradation by CMSCN5. p-C3N5 absorbs photon energy to excite e- to CB, while h+ remains in the VB. The redox potentials in semiconductor photocatalytic reactions are as follows[68] :

The oxidation potential on VB of CMSCN5 is less than Eθ(OH–/⋅OH) and Eθ(H2O/⋅OH), which cannot generate ·OH for MB degradation directly. In contrast, the CB reduction potential in CMSCN5 is more negative than Eθ(O2/⋅O2 –), and e- can reduce O2 to produce ⋅O2 – with a strong oxidizing power and thus oxidizing pollutants. In addition, the generation of ⋅OH may be obtained from O2 and H+ present in the reaction system under the action of e- [46,69,72]. The ESR signal of ⋅OOH as an important intermediate during the transformation of active radicals was also captured (Fig. 8d). Adequate 1O2 might be produced by reconstituting ⋅O2 – and the reaction of ⋅O2 – and ⋅OH[73], which corresponds to the results of the capture experiment. It can be seen from Fig. S7d shows that the addition of BQ has a more obvious inhibitory effect on MB degradation within 30 min after irradiation, and ⋅O2 – is mainly generated at this stage. However, the inhibitory effect of BQ on the degradation reaction diminishes after 30 min because of the conversion of ⋅O2 – to ⋅OH and 1O2. The photodegradation processes are described as follows:

The MoS2/CD/p-C3N5 heterostructure system not only promotes the separation of photocarriers, but also absorbs more solar energy. Multistep electronic migrations effectively separate electrons and holes and reduce the recombination rates. Thus, the synergistic effect of effective electron transfer and sufficient active sites on the composite enhances the photocatalytic efficiency.

In summary, a fungal CD/MoS2 cocatalyst system was constructed for the first time, and a D-MoS2 cocatalyst was used to combine with the semiconductor C3N5 to obtain CMSCN composite photocatalysts. Compared to previously reported single C3N5 photocatalysts[27,61], the D-MoS2 nanosheets in CMSCN composites can extract charge from the surface of C3N5, effectively improve the charge migration on the catalyst surface, and act as an active for molecular reaction sites, significantly improving the photocatalytic effect. The CMSCN5 composite had good photocatalytic H2 evolution activity, with the highest H2 evolution rate of 444 μmol h−1 g−1 when loaded with 5% D-MoS2. In addition, CMSCN5 also showed excellent activity in the adsorption and photodecomposition of MB, with a removal rate of 120 min of 93.51%. The photoelectrochemical experiments proved that CMSCN5 had a high photogenerated carrier separation ability and the best electron conductivity, which effectively improved the photocatalytic performance. A probable photocatalytic mechanism was presented, in which D-MoS2 accepted electrons and increased active sites for H2 evolution, thus preventing charge recombination and improving the H2 evolution activity. In the photocatalytic degradation pathway, 1O2 was the important active radical playing a dominant role in pollutant degradation. Therefore, CDs/MoS2/p-C3N5 composite photocatalysts have promising applications in the fields of energy development, and wastewater treatment.
